content_en:
The comparative fiqh of fasting in Bidayat al-Mujtahid illustrates how differences in hadith evaluation and linguistic interpretation produce different rules even for a practice as universally observed as Ramadan. Ibn Rushd's analysis covers the major points of scholarly disagreement with his characteristic balance of textual citation and methodological transparency.

The establishment of Ramadan's start is itself a matter of disagreement that Ibn Rushd addresses. All schools agree that Ramadan begins with the sighting of the crescent moon or the completion of thirty days of Sha'ban if the moon is not seen. The disagreement concerns the geographic scope of a valid sighting: does a moon sighting in one locality bind all Muslims globally? The majority position — accepted by all four schools in classical fiqh — is that sightings are regionally specific, and a community must sight the moon locally or receive reliable news of a nearby sighting. The astronomical calculation of the moon's position, used by some contemporary scholars to determine the start of Ramadan, was not the classical position and remains controversial.

On the intention for each day's fast: the Shafi'i and Hanbali schools require a fresh intention each night before Fajr for each day of obligatory fasting, based on the hadith: 'Whoever does not make the intention to fast before dawn has no fast' (Abu Dawud). The Maliki school agrees for obligatory fasting but allows one intention to carry through a sequence of days. The Hanafi school permits making the intention at any point before midday for an obligatory fast, based on a more flexible reading of the hadith. Ibn Rushd identifies the root as a disagreement over the temporal scope of the hadith's language.

On what breaks the fast: all schools agree that deliberate eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse break the fast. The disagreements arise over indirect causes. The Shafi'i school holds that any substance reaching the stomach through any body opening breaks the fast. The Hanafi school is more restrictive about unconventional routes of ingestion. On deliberate vomiting: the Shafi'i, Hanbali, and Maliki schools hold that deliberate vomiting of a mouthful or more breaks the fast; the Hanafi school holds it does not break the fast regardless, based on a different evaluation of the relevant hadith.

The kaffarah for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast through sexual intercourse is agreed upon as one of three expiations: freeing a slave, fasting sixty consecutive days, or feeding sixty poor people. The disagreement is whether the order of these options is fixed (obligatory hierarchy) or whether the person may choose any. The Maliki school holds the hierarchy is fixed; the person must free a slave first if able. The other three schools agree with this generally. A further disagreement concerns whether the kaffarah applies to other deliberate violations (eating and drinking): the Maliki school extends it to all deliberate violations; the other schools restrict it to sexual intercourse.

For voluntary fasting: all schools agree on the virtue of fasting Mondays and Thursdays, the day of Arafah, Ashura, and the six days of Shawwal after Ramadan. The Maliki school shows some caution about widely publicizing the six days of Shawwal lest people think they are obligatory, though it does not prohibit them. All schools agree that fasting on the two Eids is forbidden and fasting on the days of Tashriq is generally prohibited for non-pilgrims.

Ibn Rushd's comparative chapter on fasting is a model of how to navigate scholarly disagreement — acknowledging the validity of different positions while offering the reader the tools to evaluate the evidence themselves.
content_id:
Fikih komparatif puasa dalam Bidayat al-Mujtahid menggambarkan bagaimana perbedaan dalam evaluasi hadis dan penafsiran linguistik menghasilkan ketentuan-ketentuan yang berbeda bahkan untuk praktik yang dijalankan secara universal seperti Ramadan. Analisis Ibn Rushd mencakup titik-titik utama ketidaksepakatan ilmiah dengan keseimbangannya yang khas antara kutipan tekstual dan transparansi metodologis.

Penetapan awal Ramadan sendiri merupakan masalah yang diperselisihkan, yang dibahas oleh Ibn Rushd. Semua mazhab sepakat bahwa Ramadan dimulai dengan rukyat hilal atau genap tiga puluh hari Sya'ban jika bulan tidak terlihat. Perbedaannya menyangkut cakupan geografis rukyat yang sah: apakah rukyat di satu tempat mengikat seluruh Muslim secara global? Posisi mayoritas — yang diterima oleh keempat mazhab dalam fikih klasik — adalah bahwa rukyat bersifat regional, dan suatu komunitas harus merukyat hilal secara lokal atau menerima kabar yang dapat dipercaya dari rukyat di tempat yang berdekatan. Perhitungan astronomis posisi bulan, yang digunakan oleh sebagian ulama kontemporer untuk menentukan awal Ramadan, bukanlah posisi klasik dan tetap kontroversial.

Mengenai niat untuk setiap hari puasa: mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali mensyaratkan niat baru setiap malam sebelum Subuh untuk setiap hari puasa wajib, berdasarkan hadis: 'Barangsiapa tidak berniat puasa sebelum fajar maka tidak ada puasa baginya' (Abu Dawud). Mazhab Maliki sependapat untuk puasa wajib tetapi membolehkan satu niat yang berlaku untuk serangkaian hari. Mazhab Hanafi membolehkan niat kapan saja sebelum tengah hari untuk puasa wajib, berdasarkan pembacaan yang lebih fleksibel terhadap hadis. Ibn Rushd mengidentifikasi akarnya sebagai ketidaksepakatan tentang cakupan temporal bahasa hadis.

Mengenai apa yang membatalkan puasa: semua mazhab sepakat bahwa makan, minum, dan hubungan seksual yang disengaja membatalkan puasa. Perbedaan muncul mengenai sebab-sebab tidak langsung. Mazhab Syafi'i berpendapat bahwa setiap zat yang mencapai lambung melalui lubang tubuh mana pun membatalkan puasa. Mazhab Hanafi lebih ketat mengenai jalur-jalur masuk yang tidak lazim. Mengenai muntah yang disengaja: mazhab Syafi'i, Hanbali, dan Maliki berpendapat bahwa muntah yang disengaja sebanyak satu mulut penuh atau lebih membatalkan puasa; mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa hal itu tidak membatalkan puasa bagaimanapun juga, berdasarkan evaluasi yang berbeda terhadap hadis yang relevan.

Kafarat atas pelanggaran puasa Ramadan yang disengaja melalui hubungan seksual disepakati sebagai salah satu dari tiga kafarat: membebaskan budak, puasa enam puluh hari berturut-turut, atau memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin. Perbedaannya adalah apakah urutan pilihan-pilihan ini bersifat tetap (hierarki yang wajib) atau apakah seseorang boleh memilih yang mana saja. Mazhab Maliki berpendapat hierarkinya bersifat tetap; seseorang harus membebaskan budak terlebih dahulu jika mampu. Tiga mazhab lainnya umumnya sependapat dengan ini. Perbedaan lebih lanjut menyangkut apakah kafarat berlaku untuk pelanggaran disengaja lainnya (makan dan minum): mazhab Maliki memperluas kafarat ke semua pelanggaran yang disengaja; mazhab-mazhab lain membatasinya pada hubungan seksual.

Untuk puasa sunnah: semua mazhab sepakat tentang keutamaan puasa Senin dan Kamis, hari Arafah, Asyura, dan enam hari Syawwal setelah Ramadan. Mazhab Maliki menunjukkan sedikit kehati-hatian dalam mempopulerkan puasa enam hari Syawwal agar orang-orang tidak mengira puasa tersebut wajib, meskipun mazhab ini tidak melarangnya. Semua mazhab sepakat bahwa puasa pada dua Hari Raya adalah haram dan puasa pada hari-hari Tasyriq umumnya dilarang bagi selain jamaah haji.

Bab komparatif Ibn Rushd tentang puasa adalah model bagaimana menavigasi ketidaksepakatan ilmiah — mengakui validitas posisi-posisi yang berbeda sambil memberikan pembaca alat-alat untuk mengevaluasi bukti-bukti sendiri.
content_ar: The comparative الفقه of الصيام in Bidayat al-Mujtahid illustrates how differences in الحديث evaluation and linguistic interpretation produce different rules even for a practice as universally observed as Ramadan. Ibn Rushd's analysis covers the major points of العلمي disagreement with his characteristic balance of textual citation and methodological transparency. The establishment of Ramadan's start is itself a matter of disagreement that Ibn Rushd addresses. All schools agree that Ramadan begins with the sighting of the crescent moon or the completion of thirty days of Sha'ban إذا the moon is not seen. The disagreement concerns the geographic scope of a valid sighting: does a moon sighting in one locality bind all المسلمون globally? The majority position — accepted by all four schools in الكلاسيكي الفقه — is that sightings are regionally specific, and a الجماعة must sight the moon locally or receive reliable news of a nearby sighting. The astronomical calculation of the moon's position, used by some contemporary العلماء to determine the start of Ramadan, was not the الكلاسيكي position and remains controversial. On the intention for each day's fast: the الشافعي and الحنبلي schools require a fresh intention each night قبل Fajr for each day of obligatory الصيام, based on the الحديث: 'Whoever does not make the intention to fast قبل dawn has no fast' (أبو داود). The المالكي school agrees for obligatory الصيام but allows one intention to carry through a sequence of days. The الحنفي school permits making the intention at any point قبل midday for an obligatory fast, based on a more flexible reading of the الحديث. Ibn Rushd identifies the root as a disagreement over the temporal scope of the الحديث's language. On what breaks the fast: all schools agree that deliberate eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse break the fast. The disagreements arise over indirect causes. The الشافعي school holds that any substance reaching the stomach through any الجسد opening breaks the fast. The الحنفي school is more restrictive about unconventional routes of ingestion. On deliberate vomiting: the الشافعي, الحنبلي, and المالكي schools hold that deliberate vomiting of a mouthful or more breaks the fast; the الحنفي school holds it does not break the fast regardless, based on a different evaluation of the relevant الحديث. The kaffarah for deliberately breaking the Ramadan fast through sexual intercourse is agreed upon as one of three expiations: freeing a slave, الصيام sixty consecutive days, or feeding sixty poor people. The disagreement is whether the order of these options is fixed (obligatory hierarchy) or whether the person may choose any. The المالكي school holds the hierarchy is fixed; the person must free a slave أولاً إذا able. The other three schools agree with this generally.
